c) Mucus: Mucus is a thick, slimy substance produced by mucous membranes in the body. It serves as a protective barrier in various organs, including the respiratory and digestive tracts, but it does not stimulate the formation of antibodies.

d) Antibodies: Antibodies, also known as immunoglobulins, are proteins produced by the immune system in response to the presence of antigens. Antibodies specifically bind to antigens, marking them for destruction by immune cells. While antibodies are produced in response to antigens, they are not responsible for stimulating their own formation.

questions.explanation: d) Antibodies: Antibodies, also known as immunoglobulins, are proteins produced by the immune system in response to the presence of antigens. Antibodies specifically bind to antigens, marking them for destruction by immune cells. While antibodies are produced in response to antigens, they are not responsible for stimulating their own formation.

questions.text: T-lymphocytes become mature and competent under the influence of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) Liver: The liver is an important organ in the body, but it is not involved in the maturation and competency of T-lymphocytes.
b) Bursa of Fabricius: The bursa of Fabricius is an organ found in birds that plays a role in the development and maturation of B-lymphocytes. It is not associated with T-lymphocyte maturation.

c) Thymus gland: This is the correct answer. T-lymphocytes, also known as T cells, mature and become competent in the thymus gland. The thymus is a primary lymphoid organ where T cells undergo differentiation and acquire their specific functions.
d) Spleen: The spleen is an organ that filters blood and plays a role in immune responses, but it is not directly involved in the maturation of T-lymphocytes.

questions.explanation: T-lymphocytes are a type of immune cells. They originate from stem cells in the bone marrow and are sent to the thymus gland for maturation which makes them immunocompetent.

questions.text: Mucous membranes are part of body defense system and they offer:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mucous membranes are a type of epithelial tissue that lines the inside of many organs, including the respiratory tract, digestive tract, and genitourinary tract. They are part of the body's first line of defense against infection, and they offer physical barriers in several ways:The mucus layer: Mucus is a thick, sticky substance that is produced by the cells of the mucous membrane. It helps to trap bacteria and viruses, preventing them from reaching the underlying tissues.The tight junctions: The cells of the mucous membrane are held together by tight junctions, which are small gaps between the cells. These tight junctions help to prevent bacteria and viruses from passing between the cells.The cilia: The cells of the mucous membrane also have cilia, which are tiny hair-like projections. The cilia move in a coordinated fashion to help move the mucus layer along the surface of the mucous membrane. This helps to remove bacteria and viruses from the surface of the mucous membrane.
Mechanical barriers physically prevent pathogens from entering the body.
Chemical barriers kill or inhibit the growth of pathogens.

Biological barriers are made up of beneficial bacteria that live on the skin and in the mucous membranes. These bacteria help to prevent the growth of harmful bacteria by competing for nutrients and by producing antimicrobial substances.

questions.explanation: Mucous membranes act as physical barriers by trapping pathogens and preventing them from entering the body.

questions.text: The thymus gland is involved in the maturation of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Platelets are small, colorless cell fragments that are essential for blood clotting.

B-lymphocytes, also known as B cells, are a type of white blood cell that plays a vital role in the adaptive immune system. B cells are responsible for producing antibodies, which are proteins that help the body fight off infection.
Eosinophils are a type of white blood cell that plays a role in the immune system. They are called eosinophils because they have a characteristic staining pattern when viewed under a microscope.

The thymus gland is involved in the maturation of T cells, a type of white blood cell that plays a vital role in the immune system. T cells are responsible for cell-mediated immunity, which is a type of adaptive immunity that helps the body fight off infection.

questions.explanation: The thymus gland is involved in the maturation of T cells, a type of white blood cell that plays a vital role in the immune system. T cells are responsible for cell-mediated immunity, which is a type of adaptive immunity that helps the body fight off infection.

questions.text: The lymphatic vessels of the body empty the lymph into blood stream at?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: There is no specific vessel called the abdominal vein. It is a general term that refers to veins located in the abdominal region, but it does not directly receive lymph.
The jugular vein is responsible for draining blood from the head and neck, not lymph. It does not serve as the point of entry for lymph into the bloodstream.

The lymphatic vessels of the body empty the lymph into the subclavian vein. The subclavian veins are located near the collarbone and receive lymphatic fluid from the lymphatic vessels, returning it to the bloodstream.
The bile duct is a duct that carries bile from the liver to the small intestine. It is not involved in the drainage of lymph or the entry of lymph into the bloodstream.

questions.explanation: The lymphatic vessels of the body empty the lymph into the subclavian vein. The subclavian veins are located near the collarbone and receive lymphatic fluid from the lymphatic vessels, returning it to the bloodstream.

questions.text: In passive immunity, which of the following components are injected into the body?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Antigens are foreign substances that provoke an immune response. They are not injected for passive immunity, as they are primarily used in vaccines to stimulate the immune system rather than provide immediate protection.

Immunogens are substances that can induce an immune response, often overlapping with the concept of antigens. However, they are not used for passive immunity, where the goal is to deliver ready-made antibodies instead of activating the immune system.

Serum is a component of blood that can contain antibodies, but it is not specific enough to denote the direct provision of passive immunity. It may not always contain the necessary immunoglobulins needed for immediate protection.

Immunoglobulins, or antibodies, are specifically injected for passive immunity. They provide immediate protection against pathogens or toxins by neutralizing them, making them the correct choice in this context.

questions.explanation: Immunoglobulins (antibodies) are directly introduced to provide immediate immunity.

questions.text: In the structural diagram of an antibody molecule which portion is occupied by variable chains?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The lower region of an antibody molecule is known as the constant region, which does not contain variable chains. It is involved in effector functions.

This option is correct. The upper region of the antibody molecule consists of variable chains, including both the variable heavy chain and the variable light chains. This region, also known as the antigen-binding region, determines the antibody's specificity and its ability to bind to different antigens.
This option is incorrect. The middle region of an antibody does not specifically refer to the location of variable chains, as they are primarily found in the upper region of the structure.

This option is incorrect. The term 'in between chains' does not accurately describe a specific region where variable chains are located. The variable chains are situated in the upper region of the antibody structure.

questions.explanation: The variable chains of an antibody molecule, both the variable heavy chain and the variable light chains, occupy the upper region of the structural diagram. The region is also known as the antigen-binding region. It is responsible for the specificity of the antibody and its ability to bind to different antigens. The lower region of the antibody, known as the constant region, does not contain the variable chains.

questions.text: In E.C.G QRS complex represent:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The atrial systole is represented by the P wave, not the QRS complex. The P wave corresponds to the depolarization of the atria, leading to atrial contraction.

Atrial diastole is the relaxation phase of the atria, which is not represented by the QRS complex. The atria repolarize during the QRS complex, but this is not visible in the ECG due to the dominance of the ventricular signal.

The QRS complex represents the depolarization of the ventricles, which is immediately followed by ventricular systole (contraction). This is the primary electrical activity observed during the QRS complex.
The T wave follows the QRS complex and represents ventricular repolarization, corresponding to ventricular diastole (relaxation), not the QRS complex.

questions.explanation: The second part of ECG is the QRS complex which features a small drop in voltage (Q) a large voltage peak ( R ) and another small drop in voltage (S). The QRS complex corresponds to the depolarization of the ventricle during ventricle systole.

questions.text: Purkinje fibers are connected with the impulse conducting system of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Correct! Purkinje fibers are integral to the heart's impulse conducting system. They swiftly transmit electrical impulses, ensuring synchronized contraction of the ventricles for efficient blood pumping.

Incorrect. The brain's impulse conducting system is composed of neurons, not Purkinje fibers. These neurons are responsible for transmitting electrical signals within the brain and to the rest of the body.
Incorrect. The skin's impulse conducting system consists of nerves that transmit sensory information to the brain, and it does not involve Purkinje fibers.
Incorrect. The nephron's impulse conducting system involves epithelial cells for ion and water transport, with no role for Purkinje fibers.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: Heart. Purkinje fibers are a critical component of the heart's impulse conducting system. They rapidly transmit electrical impulses, which orchestrate the nearly simultaneous contraction of the ventricles, facilitating efficient blood circulation. In contrast, the brain relies on neurons for impulse transmission, the skin uses nerve endings for sensory signaling, and the nephron uses epithelial cells for its physiological processes.

questions.text: In roots the apoplast pathway of water is disrupted when water reaches:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plasmodesmata are channels that connect the cytoplasm of adjacent plant cells and are not directly related to disrupting the apoplast pathway in roots.
The cortex is the outer tissue layer of the root and is not directly responsible for disrupting the apoplast pathway.

This option is correct. In roots, the apoplast pathway of water is disrupted when water reaches the endodermis. The endodermis is a specialized layer of cells in the root that is characterized by the presence of the Casparian strip, a waxy barrier made of suberin. The Casparian strip blocks the apoplastic movement of water and minerals, forcing substances to enter the symplast pathway by crossing the cell membranes of the endodermal cells.
The pith is the central tissue region of a stem, and it is not directly relevant to disrupting the apoplast pathway in roots.

questions.explanation: In roots, the apoplast pathway of water is disrupted when water reaches the endodermis. The endodermis is a specialized layer of cells in the root that is characterized by the presence of the Casparian strip, a waxy barrier made of suberin. The Casparian strip blocks the apoplastic movement of water and minerals, forcing substances to enter the symplast pathway by crossing the cell membranes of the endodermal cells.

questions.text: T-lymphocytcs recognize antigen and attack microorganisms or transplanted organ and tissues. The effect is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cell-mediated immunity or cellular immunity is an immune response that does not involve antibodies. Rather, cell-mediated immunity is the activation of phagocytes, antigen-specific cytotoxic T-lymphocytes, and the release of various cytokines in response to an antigen.

Active Immunity results when exposure to a disease organism triggers the immune system to produce antibodies to that disease. Active immunity can be acquired through natural immunity or vaccine-induced immunity.

Humoral immunity is the aspect of immunity that is mediated by macromolecules - including secreted antibodies, complement proteins, and certain antimicrobial peptides - located in extracellular fluids. Humoral immunity is named so because it involves substances found in the humors, or body fluids.

Passive immunity is provided when a person is given antibodies to a disease rather than producing them through his or her own immune system. A newborn baby acquires passive immunity from its mother through the placenta.

questions.explanation: The humoral immune system deals with antigens from pathogen, that are freely circulating, or outside the infected cells. Cellular immunity occurs inside infected cells and is mediated by T lymphocytes.

questions.text: Now a days, every new born gets regular shots of vaccine for polio. It contains _ for polio to make a child immune against this disease.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Antisera contains antibodies but are not components of vaccines. They are used for passive immunity, not in preventive vaccines like polio.
Antibiotics are used to treat bacterial infections and are not related to vaccines, which are meant to prevent viral infections like polio.
Antibodies are produced by the immune system in response to vaccines, but they are not components of the vaccine itself.
Antigens in vaccines are derived from the pathogen (like the poliovirus) and are used to stimulate the immune system to produce antibodies, leading to immunity.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer is D) Antigens, as polio vaccines contain antigens from the poliovirus to induce immunity against the disease. Two distinct antigenic forms of poliovirus exist, designated D and C antigens (Mayer et al., 1957), sometimes referred to as N and H antigens. D antigen is expressed on native infectious viruses and C antigen on non-infectious empty particles.

questions.text: Cell turgidity is caused by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Endosmosis is a type of osmosis, which is defined as the movement of water across a semipermeable membrane. It causes the cell to become turgid.
Exosmosis is the outward movement of water from a cell through a semipermeable membrane.
Plasmolysis is the process in which cells lose water when they are placed in a hypertonic solution. It causes contraction or shrinking of the plasma membrane away from the cell wall.

In cellular biology, active transport is the movement of molecules or ions across a cell membrane from a region of lower concentration to a region of higher concentration—against the concentration gradient.

questions.explanation: Turgidity refers to the state of a plant cell when it is filled with water and the cell membrane pushes against the cell wall, creating internal pressure. This pressure is essential for maintaining the structural integrity and rigidity of plant cells. Endosmosis is a process by which water molecules move into the cell from an area of lower solute concentration (hypotonic solution) to an area of higher solute concentration (inside the cell). This movement of water occurs across a selectively permeable membrane, such as the cell membrane, and is driven by the concentration gradient of solutes between the cell and its surroundings. Hence, option A is correct.

questions.text: Lymphoid masses present in the wall of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These lymphoid masses in the digestive tract contain specialized immune cells, such as lymphocytes, plasma cells, and macrophages, which collectively help identify and neutralize pathogens, toxins, and other harmful substances that may enter the digestive system through ingested food and water. Therefore option A is correct.
The submucosa is a thick layer of loose connective tissue that surrounds the mucosa. This layer also contains blood vessels, lymphatic vessels, and nerves. Glands may be embedded in this layer
Mucosa is another name for mucous membrane. Mucosa lines the insides of organs and cavities throughout your body that are exposed to particles from the outside.
This is not correct option

questions.explanation: These lymphoid masses in the digestive tract contain specialized immune cells, such as lymphocytes, plasma cells, and macrophages, which collectively help identify and neutralize pathogens, toxins, and other harmful substances that may enter the digestive system through ingested food and water. Therefore option A is correct.

questions.text: Lymph capillaries join together form larger lymph vessels that gives rise to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The general flow of lymph is towards the thoracic duct. However when the small lymph capillaries join together, they form a larger lymph vessel that is called the lymph duct.
Lymph duct is the one which is comprised from multiple small lymph capillaries joining together.

The bile duct is a duct system that plays a crucial role in the digestive process by transporting bile from the liver and gallbladder to the small intestine. Bile is a greenish-yellow fluid that contains bile salts, bile pigments, cholesterol, and other substances necessary for the digestion and absorption of fats.

The sperm duct is a long muscular tube that extends from each epididymis, one on each side, and ascends through the spermatic cord. The spermatic cord is a structure that contains the vas deferens, blood vessels, nerves, and other structures that support the testes. The two vas deferens join together at the base of the prostate gland to form the ejaculatory ducts, which then empty into the urethra.

questions.explanation: Lymph duct is the one which is comprised from multiple small lymph capillaries joining together, therefore option B is correct.

questions.text: Amount of lymph produced per day is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct amount.
This is more than the average amount.
This is more than the average amount.
This statement states that the above options are incorrect, however that is not correct as option A is correct.

questions.explanation: The body produces about 2-3 liters of lymph per day.

questions.text: Pathogens inside the body are killed by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Antibodies are proteins that are produced by the immune system to fight infection. They bind to specific antigens on the surface of pathogens, which helps to mark them for destruction by the immune system. Immune system cells, such as phagocytes and B cells, also play a role in killing pathogens.
Phagocytes engulf and digest pathogens, while B cells produce antibodies.
Interferon is a protein that is produced by cells that are infected with viruses. It helps to protect other cells from being infected by the virus.

The immune system is a complex system that works to protect the body from infection. It uses a variety of mechanisms to kill pathogens, including antibodies, immune system cells, interferon, the complement system, natural killer cells, and cytokines.

questions.explanation: The immune system is a complex system that works to protect the body from infection. It uses a variety of mechanisms to kill pathogens, including antibodies, immune system cells, interferon, the complement system, natural killer cells, and cytokines.

questions.text: What is true about T-Cells?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is correct.

options.text: Present in blood and work as defence
options.explanation: This statement is correct.
This statement is correct.

All of the statements about T-cells are true. T-cells are a type of lymphocyte, which are white blood cells that play a vital role in the immune system. They are present in the blood and lymph, and they help to fight infection by killing foreign invaders.

questions.explanation: All of the statements about T-cells are true. T-cells are a type of lymphocyte, which are white blood cells that play a vital role in the immune system. They are present in the blood and lymph, and they help to fight infection by killing foreign invaders.

questions.text: Iliac arteries supply blood to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The stomach is primarily supplied by the celiac artery (celiac trunk), which is a branch of the abdominal aorta. The celiac artery provides blood to the upper abdominal organs, including the stomach.

The large intestine is primarily supplied by the superior mesenteric artery, another branch of the abdominal aorta. The superior mesenteric artery provides blood to the midgut, which includes portions of the small intestine and the large intestine.

The esophagus receives its blood supply from various arteries, including branches of the thoracic aorta, the bronchial arteries, and the left gastric artery. It is not directly supplied by the iliac arteries.

The iliac arteries, specifically the internal iliac arteries, provide blood supply to the pelvic region, including the reproductive organs (gonads) such as the ovaries in females and the testes in males.

questions.explanation: The iliac arteries, specifically the internal iliac arteries, provide blood supply to the pelvic region, including the reproductive organs (gonads) such as the ovaries in females and the testes in males.

questions.text: Heart is enclosed in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pericardium is a double-walled sac that surrounds the heart. It consists of an outer fibrous pericardium and an inner serous pericardium. The pericardium provides protection for the heart, anchors it in the chest, and prevents overexpansion.

The pleural membrane is not directly associated with the heart; it surrounds the lungs. It consists of the visceral pleura (which covers the lungs) and the parietal pleura (which lines the chest cavity).
The mesentery is a fold of the peritoneum that connects the intestines to the abdominal wall. It is not related to the heart's enclosure.
The epimysium is a layer of connective tissue that surrounds individual skeletal muscles, not the heart.

questions.explanation: The pericardium is a double-walled sac that surrounds the heart. It consists of an outer fibrous pericardium and an inner serous pericardium. The pericardium provides protection for the heart, anchors it in the chest, and prevents overexpansion.

questions.text: Papillary muscles extension are responsible for:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Papillary muscles and their extensions are responsible for constricting (or tightening) the chordae tendineae associated with the bicuspid (mitral) valve in the left ventricle of the heart. This prevents the valve from prolapsing (bulging back) into the left atrium when the ventricle contracts.

Similarly, papillary muscles and their extensions also play a role in constricting the chordae tendineae associated with the tricuspid valve in the right ventricle. This prevents the tricuspid valve from prolapsing when the right ventricle contracts.

Mitral constriction is essentially the same as bicuspid constriction. The mitral valve is the left atrioventricular valve, also known as the bicuspid valve. Papillary muscles and their extensions help prevent mitral (bicuspid) valve prolapse.

The correct answer is "all of these" because papillary muscles and their extensions are responsible for preventing the prolapse of both the mitral (bicuspid) and tricuspid valves in the heart. They contribute to the proper functioning of these valves during the cardiac cycle.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is "all of these" because papillary muscles and their extensions are responsible for preventing the prolapse of both the mitral (bicuspid) and tricuspid valves in the heart. They contribute to the proper functioning of these valves during the cardiac cycle.

questions.text: A circulatory system has ------- characteristics.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not accurate. Circulatory systems typically have more than one characteristic. In fact, the term "circulatory system" implies a complex set of functions and structures for transporting substances (such as blood) throughout an organism's body.

While circulatory systems can have various components and functions, they are typically characterized by more than two key characteristics. For instance, a circulatory system includes the pumping organ (heart), a network of vessels (arteries, veins, capillaries), and the circulatory fluid (e.g., blood or hemolymph).

This is the correct answer. A circulatory system typically has three key characteristics:)>A pumping organ (e.g., heart or equivalent) to propel the circulatory fluid.)>A network of vessels that transport the circulatory fluid.)>The circulatory fluid itself (e.g., blood or hemolymph) that carries substances throughout the organism's body.
Six characteristics do not specifically define a circulatory system. Circulatory systems are typically described by the three key characteristics mentioned above.

questions.explanation: A circulatory system typically has three key characteristics:)>A pumping organ (e.g., heart or equivalent) to propel the circulatory fluid.)>A network of vessels that transport the circulatory fluid.)>The circulatory fluid itself (e.g., blood or hemolymph) that carries substances throughout the organism's body.

questions.text: Blood is collected from legs by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The hepatic vein carries blood from the liver to the inferior vena cava but is not involved in collecting blood from the legs.

The vena cava (superior and inferior) carries deoxygenated blood from various parts of the body to the right atrium of the heart. While it plays a role in returning blood to the heart, it receives blood from multiple sources, not just the legs.
Renal veins carry blood away from the kidneys, not from the legs.

The iliac veins, including the common iliac veins and external and internal iliac veins, collect blood from the lower limbs (legs) and return it to the inferior vena cava, which then transports it to the right atrium of the heart. These veins are specifically responsible for draining blood from the legs.

questions.explanation: "The explanation for this question will be added soon!".

questions.text: Which one is thickest?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The left ventricle is the thickest chamber of the heart because it has to generate the force required to pump oxygenated blood throughout the entire body, overcoming systemic vascular resistance. This muscle is thicker and more powerful than the right ventricle.
The right ventricle has a thinner muscle wall compared to the left ventricle. Its primary function is to pump deoxygenated blood to the lungs for oxygenation.

The left atrium is one of the upper chambers of the heart and has thinner walls compared to the ventricles. Its role is to receive oxygenated blood from the pulmonary veins and pump it into the left ventricle.

Similar to the left atrium, the right atrium is also one of the upper chambers of the heart and has thinner walls compared to the ventricles. Its function is to receive deoxygenated blood from the body's systemic circulation and deliver it to the right ventricle.

questions.explanation: The left ventricle is the thickest chamber of the heart because it has to generate the force required to pump oxygenated blood throughout the entire body, overcoming systemic vascular resistance. This muscle is thicker and more powerful than the right ventricle.

questions.text: A 25 years old female with chronic fatigue was diagnosed with iron deficiency anemia and low blood count. What is the cause of her fatigue?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Reduction in amount of Fe-S centers
options.explanation: This is not correctIron is a key component of cytochromes and iron–sulfur (Fe–S) proteins in the ETC.

options.text: Lowered production of water from the electron transport chain that cause dehydration
options.explanation: This is not correctIron is a key component of cytochromes and iron–sulfur (Fe–S) proteins in the ETC.

options.text: Iron is important for electron transport chain
options.explanation: Iron is a key component of cytochromes and iron–sulfur (Fe–S) proteins in the ETC.In these molecules, iron alternates between Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺ states to transfer electrons along the chainThis electron flow drives the pumping of protons across the mitochondrial membrane, which powers ATP synthesis.ETC activity decreases → less ATP production → fatigue worsens (in addition to low oxygen transport from anaemia).

options.text: Iron is important for NADH production
options.explanation: This is not correctIron is a key component of cytochromes and iron–sulfur (Fe–S) proteins in the ETC.

questions.explanation: Option C is the correct option because Fe-S clusters mediate electron transfer within and between the respiratory complexes of the electron transport chain so iron is important for the electron transport chain to continue and for ATP synthesis.Option A is not correct because the reduction in the amount of Fe-S centers doesn't lead to low blood count.Option B is not correct because lower production of water does not lead to anemia.Option D is not correct because NADH is a crucial coenzyme in making ATP; it acts as a hydrogen acceptor in an oxidation-reduction reaction and does not require Iron.

questions.text: The function of spleen is to filter_.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The primary function of the spleen is to filter and cleanse the blood. It plays a crucial role in removing damaged red blood cells, pathogens, and cellular debris from the bloodstream. Additionally, the spleen stores platelets and is involved in immune responses.
The spleen is not involved in the filtration of amniotic fluid. This fluid surrounds and protects the developing fetus during pregnancy and is not processed by the spleen.
The spleen has no role in filtering or processing semen. Semen is a reproductive fluid produced by the male reproductive system.

While the spleen is part of the lymphatic system and plays a role in immune responses, it primarily filters blood, not lymph. Lymph nodes and other lymphatic organs are responsible for filtering lymphatic fluid.

questions.explanation: The primary function of the spleen is to filter and cleanse the blood. It plays a crucial role in removing damaged red blood cells, pathogens, and cellular debris from the bloodstream. Additionally, the spleen stores platelets and is involved in immune responses.

questions.text: The number of stages involved in the heart beat is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While the heart's function is often simplified into two phases (systole and diastole), these phases are part of a more detailed three-stage cycle.
The heartbeat consists of three stages: atrial systole, ventricular systole, and complete cardiac diastole.
Although detailed descriptions may sometimes include additional steps for educational purposes, the standard division of the heartbeat is into three stages.
There is no standard model of the human heartbeat that includes five stages.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Three'. The heartbeat is divided into three main stages: atrial systole, ventricular systole, and complete cardiac diastole. These stages ensure the coordinated contraction and relaxation of the heart chambers, allowing efficient blood flow through the heart and to the rest of the body. Option A is incorrect because it oversimplifies the cardiac cycle into two phases, omitting the detailed breakdown. Option C is incorrect as it adds unnecessary complexity not recognized in standard medical literature. Option D is incorrect because a five-stage model is not a standard description of the human heartbeat.

questions.text: The osmotic pressure of blood Is maintained by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Membrane proteins are common proteins that are part of, or interact with, biological membranes

Fibrous proteins contain polypeptide chains organized approximately in parallel along a single axis, producing long fibers or large sheets. Such proteins tend to be mechanically strong and resistant to solubilization in water. Fibrous proteins often play a structural role in nature.

Serum albumin is a protein found in the blood plasma which help in the maintainance of osmotic pressure between the blood vessels and tissues. Serum albumin accounts for 55 percent of the total protein in blood plasma.
Myosin is a contractile protein which helps in the contraction of muscles.

questions.explanation: Serum albumin, protein found in blood plasma that helps maintain the osmotic pressure between the blood vessels and tissues. Serum albumin accounts for 55 percent of the total protein in blood plasma.

questions.text: Pressure flow theory was proposed by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ernst Münch, a German plant physiologist, proposed the pressure flow theory in the 1920s. This theory explains how the movement of organic compounds, primarily sugars, occurs in the phloem of plants, emphasizing the role of pressure gradients in this process.

C.B. van Niel was a Dutch-American microbiologist and biochemist who made significant contributions to our understanding of photosynthesis and the role of microorganisms in the carbon cycle. He is not associated with the pressure flow theory.

Hans Krebs was a German-British biochemist who is well-known for his work on the citric acid cycle (also known as the Krebs cycle) in cellular respiration. He is not credited with the pressure flow theory in plant physiology.

Thomas Hunt Morgan was an American geneticist and biologist known for his work in genetics, particularly related to the fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster. He is not associated with the pressure flow theory.

questions.explanation: Ernst Münch, a German plant physiologist, proposed the pressure flow theory in the 1920s. This theory explains how the movement of organic compounds, primarily sugars, occurs in the phloem of plants, emphasizing the role of pressure gradients in this process.

questions.text: Guttation is caused due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Guttation is not caused by the suppression of transpiration but rather occurs when transpiration is minimal or reduced, typically at night when there is a high soil water content.
Guttation is not directly caused by humidity. It is more closely related to the movement of water and minerals from the roots into the xylem, resulting in increased root pressure.

Guttation occurs when root pressure in plant roots forces excess water and dissolved minerals into the plant's vascular system. This excess water is then exuded from hydathodes on the leaf margins in the form of liquid droplets.
The correct cause of guttation is root pressure, as mentioned in option c.

questions.explanation: Guttation occurs when root pressure in plant roots forces excess water and dissolved minerals into the plant's vascular system. This excess water is then exuded from hydathodes on the leaf margins in the form of liquid droplets.

questions.text: Multisensory hydraulic valves are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Stomata are small pores found on the surface of plant leaves and stems. They regulate gas exchange, allowing water vapor to exit the leaf during transpiration and enabling the entry of carbon dioxide for photosynthesis. Stomata are not multisensory hydraulic valves.

Lenticels are small, corky, porous structures found on the surface of woody stems and branches. They serve as ports for gas exchange, allowing for the exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide between the inner, living tissues and the external environment. Lenticels can be considered multisensory hydraulic valves due to their role in facilitating gas exchange.

Guard cells are specialized cells found in the epidermis of plant leaves and stems. They control the opening and closing of stomata, regulating gas exchange and water loss, but they are not typically referred to as multisensory hydraulic valves.

Hydathodes are specialized structures located at the leaf margins or tips, and they play a role in exuding liquid (guttation) from plant leaves. They are not commonly referred to as multisensory hydraulic valves.

questions.explanation: Lenticels are small, corky, porous structures found on the surface of woody stems and branches. They serve as ports for gas exchange, allowing for the exchange of oxygen and carbon dioxide between the inner, living tissues and the external environment. Lenticels can be considered multisensory hydraulic valves due to their role in facilitating gas exchange.

questions.text: The absorption of water through a compound without dissolving in it is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ascent of sap refers to the upward movement of water and minerals in a plant from the roots to the aerial parts, driven by transpiration and water potential gradients.
Plasmolysis is the shrinking or contraction of the protoplast (cell contents) away from the cell wall due to the loss of water, often occurring in hypertonic conditions.

Imbibition is the process by which substances, especially water, are absorbed by and swell within the internal structure of a solid, such as seeds, cell walls, or other materials. Imbibition is common in processes like seed germination and hydration of plant tissues.

Guttation is the exudation of water from plant leaves, typically at night, through specialized structures called hydathodes. It is different from imbibition, as guttation involves the loss of water from the plant's leaves, not the absorption of water into a solid compound.

questions.explanation: Imbibition is the process by which substances, especially water, are absorbed by and swell within the internal structure of a solid, such as seeds, cell walls, or other materials. Imbibition is common in processes like seed germination and hydration of plant tissues.

questions.text: Water vapors exit and carbon dioxide enters a leaf through:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Stomata are small pores found on the surface of plant leaves and stems. They regulate gas exchange, allowing water vapor to exit the leaf during transpiration and enabling the entry of carbon dioxide for photosynthesis.
Grana are structures within chloroplasts where photosynthesis takes place. They are involved in the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis and are not directly responsible for gas exchange.
The porphyrin ring is a component of chlorophyll molecules and is critical for capturing light energy during photosynthesis. It is not directly related to gas exchange.

Photons are packets of light energy, and they are involved in the process of photosynthesis, specifically in the conversion of light energy into chemical energy. While they play a role in photosynthesis, they are not responsible for gas exchange in leaves.

questions.explanation: Stomata are small pores found on the surface of plant leaves and stems. They regulate gas exchange, allowing water vapor to exit the leaf during transpiration and enabling the entry of carbon dioxide for photosynthesis.

questions.text: Which cells regulate the opening and closing of stomata?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Neutrophils are a type of white blood cell (leukocyte) that play a key role in the immune system's response to infections. They are not involved in the regulation of stomata in plant leaves.
Basophils are another type of white blood cell involved in immune responses, particularly allergic reactions. They are not related to the regulation of stomata.

Guard cells are specialized cells found in the epidermis of plant leaves and stems. They control the opening and closing of stomata, small pores in the leaf surface, by changing their turgor pressure in response to environmental and physiological cues.

Mesophyll cells are the primary photosynthetic cells in plant leaves. While they play a vital role in photosynthesis, they do not have the specific function of regulating stomatal opening and closing.

questions.explanation: Guard cells are specialized cells found in the epidermis of plant leaves and stems. They control the opening and closing of stomata, small pores in the leaf surface, by changing their turgor pressure in response to environmental and physiological cues.

questions.text: Stomata cover only what portion of the leaf surface?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Stomata do not cover as much as 10% of the leaf surface. They are relatively small openings and are not distributed so densely.
Stomata do not cover half (50%) of the leaf surface. They are present in smaller numbers and are not evenly distributed to account for such a large portion.

Stomata typically cover a relatively small portion of the leaf surface, usually in the range of 1-2%. This percentage can vary depending on the plant species, environmental conditions, and leaf characteristics.

This is a more specific estimate, and while it might be accurate for some plant species, the typical range for stomatal coverage is more commonly cited as 1-2%. Stomata are relatively small openings on the leaf's surface and are not as densely distributed as other leaf structures.

questions.explanation: Stomata typically cover a relatively small portion of the leaf surface, usually in the range of 1-2%. This percentage can vary depending on the plant species, environmental conditions, and leaf characteristics.

questions.text: The opening of the larynx is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nostrils are the external openings of the nasal passages, not the larynx.
The glottis is the opening between the vocal cords in the larynx, which is responsible for producing sound and regulating the passage of air into the trachea.
Nares are another term for nostrils, which are part of the nasal passages, not the larynx.

The epiglottis is a flap-like structure in the throat that helps prevent food and liquids from entering the trachea during swallowing. It is located above the larynx but is not the opening of the larynx itself.

questions.explanation: The opening of the larynx is called the "glottis." The glottis is the space between the vocal cords, and it plays a crucial role in phonation (sound production) and breathing. The vocal cords can open and close to modify the size of the glottis, which affects the pitch and volume of the sounds produced when air passes through the larynx.

questions.text: The Venus-flower basket is also known as which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Sycon is a genus of calcareous sponges belonging to the family Sycettidae. It is not the Venus-flower basket.
This is incorrect. Leucosolenia is a genus of calcareous sponges in the family Leucosoleniidae. It is also not the Venus-flower basket.
This is incorrect. Spongilla is a genus of freshwater sponges, not related to the Venus-flower basket.
This is correct. Euplectella is the binomial name for the Venus-flower basket. It is a delicate marine sponge with a glassy skeleton, often found in deep-sea habitats.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct since the binomial name for the Venus flower basket is Euplectella. Option A is incorrect because 'Sycon' is a genus of calcareous sponges belonging to the family Sycettidae. Option B is incorrect since 'Leucosolenia' is a genus of calcareous sponges belonging to the family Leucosoleniidae. Option C is incorrect since 'Spongilla' is a genus of freshwater sponges and has a leuconoid body form with a skeleton composed of siliceous spicules.

questions.text: In an antibody molecule two heavy chains and two light chains are bonded by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Two light chains are connected to heavy chains by one disulphide bond each. Two heavy chains are connected by two disulphide bonds
Hydrogen bonds are not present in antibody molecule.
Phosphodiester bonds are not present in antibody molecule.
Ionic bonds are also absent in antibody molecule.

questions.explanation: An antibody molecule consists of four polypeptide chains—two identical light chain and two identical heavy chains—linked by disulfide (-S—S-) bridges

questions.text: Antibodies consists of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: 2 light chains and 2 heavy chains arranged in a Y-shaped configuration
options.explanation: This is the correct structure of antibodies. Antibodies, also known as immunoglobulins, consist of two identical light chains and two identical heavy chains, forming a Y-shaped molecule.

options.text: A light chain and 2 heavy chains arranged in a Y-shaped configuration
options.explanation: This description is not accurate. Antibodies have two light chains and two heavy chains, not a single light chain.

options.text: 2 light chains and a heavy chain arranged in a Y-shaped configuration
options.explanation: This option does not accurately represent the structure of antibodies. Antibodies consist of two light chains and two heavy chains.
The structure of antibodies is specifically characterized by the arrangement of two light chains and two heavy chains in a Y-shaped configuration.

questions.explanation: Option A is the correct structure of antibodies. Antibodies, also known as immunoglobulins, consist of two identical light chains and two identical heavy chains, forming a Y-shaped molecule.

questions.text: Passive immune response is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Passive immunity, whether natural or artificial, is not long-lasting. It provides short-term protection because it involves the transfer of pre-formed antibodies rather than the development of the individual's immune memory.
Passive immunity is not delayed; it provides immediate protection. The individual receives pre-formed antibodies or immune components, which are already effective against the target pathogen.
Passive immunity offers immediate protection because it involves the direct transfer of ready-made antibodies or immune components to the individual. There is no delay in the immune response.
Passive immunity is not persistent; it gradually wanes as the transferred antibodies or immune components are cleared from the recipient's system. It does not provide long-term protection.

questions.explanation: Passive immunity offers immediate protection because it involves the direct transfer of ready-made antibodies or immune components to the individual. There is no delay in the immune response.

questions.text: In a _ solution solute and pressure potentials are equal to water potential.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This term is not commonly used in biology and is not a recognized type of solution.

This option is correct. In an isotonic solution, the solute potential and pressure potential are equal to the water potential. An isotonic solution has the same concentration of solutes as the solution inside the cell, resulting in no net movement of water. As a result, the water potential inside the cell is equal to the water potential outside the cell.
In a hypertonic solution, the solute potential is lower and the water potential is lower than that of the cell. Water moves out of the cell, causing it to lose water and shrink.
In a hypotonic solution, the solute potential is higher and the water potential is higher than that of the cell. Water moves into the cell, causing it to gain water and become turgid.

questions.explanation: In an isotonic solution, the solute potential and pressure potential are equal to the water potential. An isotonic solution has the same concentration of solutes as the solution inside the cell, resulting in no net movement of water. As a result, the water potential inside the cell is equal to the water potential outside the cell.

questions.text: Apoplast pathway can take water and minerals up to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The apoplast pathway is a route for water and minerals to move through the non-living spaces of plant tissues, such as the cell walls. While the apoplast pathway can transport water and minerals through the cortex and endodermis, it is stopped by the Casparian strip in the endodermis, preventing direct entry into the xylem. Therefore, this option is not entirely correct.

The apoplast pathway refers to movement through cell walls and non-living spaces. While the cortex is part of the plant tissue, the apoplast pathway extends beyond the cortex to the endodermis. The Casparian strip in the endodermis prevents further apoplastic movement beyond this point. Therefore, this option is not entirely correct.

Correct. The apoplast pathway can transport water and minerals up to the endodermis, which is a specialized layer of cells in the root. However, the Casparian strip in the endodermis acts as a barrier, stopping the apoplast pathway from continuing into the xylem. Water and minerals must enter the symplast pathway by crossing the endodermal cell membranes to reach the xylem.

The apoplast pathway can extend up to the pericycle, a tissue layer just inside the endodermis. However, it is important to note that the apoplast pathway is stopped by the Casparian strip in the endodermis, preventing direct transport of water and minerals from the pericycle to the xylem. Therefore, this option is not entirely correct.

questions.explanation: The apoplast pathway can transport water and minerals up to the endodermis, which is a specialized layer of cells in the root. However, the Casparian strip in the endodermis acts as a barrier, stopping the apoplast pathway from continuing into the xylem. Water and minerals must enter the symplast pathway by crossing the endodermal cell membranes to reach the xylem.

questions.text: In plants the neighboring cells are connected with one another by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct. In plants, neighboring cells are connected with one another by specialized channels called plasmodesmata. Plasmodesmata are microscopic channels that pass through the cell walls and connect the cytoplasm of adjacent plant cells, allowing for communication, transport of water, nutrients, and other molecules between cells.
While cell walls provide structural support and protection for plant cells, they do not directly connect neighboring cells. Plasmodesmata are the structures responsible for connecting plant cells.
Vacuoles are storage organelles within plant cells and are not involved in directly connecting neighboring cells.
Casparian strips are waxy barriers found in the endodermis of roots, and they prevent the movement of substances through the apoplast pathway in roots. They do not connect neighboring cells.

questions.explanation: In plants, neighboring cells are connected with one another by specialized channels called plasmodesmata. Plasmodesmata are microscopic channels that pass through the cell walls and connect the cytoplasm of adjacent plant cells, allowing for communication, transport of water, nutrients, and other molecules between cells.

questions.text: Water and minerals move down their concentration gradient through plasmadesmata to cells of cortex endodermis pericycle and then to sap in the xylem cells.This is also known as the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct. The movement of water and minerals down their concentration gradient through plasmodesmata, from cell to cell in the cortex, endodermis, pericycle, and ultimately to the sap in the xylem cells, is known as the symplast pathway. In the symplast pathway, substances move through the living parts of cells, such as the cytoplasm and plasmodesmata.

The vacuolar pathway is the movement of water molecules in plant cells via the vacuoles located in the cytoplasm of the cell but it is not used to describe the movement of water and minerals through plasmodesmata and cell layers..
While minerals are absorbed by plant roots, the term "mineral absorption pathway" is not commonly used to describe the specific movement of water and minerals through plasmodesmata and cell layers.
The apoplast pathway refers to the movement of water and minerals through the non-living spaces of cell walls and is not directly related to the symplast pathway.

questions.explanation: The movement of water and minerals down their concentration gradient through plasmodesmata, from cell to cell in the cortex, endodermis, pericycle, and ultimately to the sap in the xylem cells, is known as the symplast pathway. In the symplast pathway, substances move through the living parts of cells, such as the cytoplasm and plasmodesmata.

questions.text: In the root cells _ pathway becomes discontinuous in the endodermis due to the presence of casparian strips.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The tonoplast is the membrane surrounding the vacuole and is not directly related to the discontinuity of the apoplast pathway in the endodermis.

This option is correct. In the root cells, the apoplast pathway becomes discontinuous in the endodermis due to the presence of Casparian strips. The Casparian strips are waxy barriers made of suberin that are present in the radial and transverse walls of endodermal cells. They block the apoplastic movement of water, nutrients, and ions, forcing substances to enter the symplast pathway by crossing the cell membranes of the endodermal cells.
The symplast pathway involves movement through the living parts of cells and is not directly affected by the presence of Casparian strips.

The vacuolar pathway is the movement of water molecules in plant cells via the vacuoles located in the cytoplasm of the cell but it is not related to the discontinuity of the apoplast pathway in the endodermis.

questions.explanation: In the root cells, the apoplast pathway becomes discontinuous in the endodermis due to the presence of Casparian strips. The Casparian strips are waxy barriers made of suberin that are present in the radial and transverse walls of endodermal cells. They block the apoplastic movement of water, nutrients, and ions, forcing substances to enter the symplast pathway by crossing the cell membranes of the endodermal cells.

questions.text: Chordae tendinae are extension of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, the chordae tendinae are not extensions of the aorta. The aorta functions as the major artery transporting oxygenated blood from the heart to the body, whereas the chordae tendinae are involved in valve function within the heart.

No, chordae tendinae are not extensions of the ventricles themselves, though they are located within. They connect the valve leaflets to the papillary muscles, serving a functional role in preventing valve inversion during ventricular contraction.

No, chordae tendinae are not extensions of the auricles. Auricles are ear-shaped structures on the atria and do not participate in the structural support of the atrioventricular valves like the chordae tendinae do.

Correct. Chordae tendinae are extensions of the atrioventricular valves. They attach these valves to the papillary muscles, playing a crucial role in maintaining valve function and ensuring one-way blood flow during heart contractions.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct. Chordae tendinae are fibrous cords that extend from the atrioventricular valves to the papillary muscles in the ventricles. Their primary role is to maintain valve closure during ventricular contraction, preventing backflow into the atria. If damaged, valve integrity is compromised, potentially leading to conditions such as valve regurgitation. The other options—Aorta, Ventricles, and Auricles—do not directly involve the chordae tendinae in their structures or functions.

questions.text: Mitral valve prevents the flow of blood from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, the mitral valve does not prevent the flow of blood from the right atrium to the right ventricle. This valve is located on the left side of the heart, between the left atrium and the left ventricle, not on the right side.
No, the mitral valve allows blood to flow from the left atrium to the left ventricle, not preventing it. Its main role is to prevent backflow to the left atrium.
No, the mitral valve is not involved in the flow between the right atrium and the left atrium. It is situated between the left atrium and the left ventricle.
Correct. The mitral valve prevents the backflow of blood from the left ventricle to the left atrium during ventricular contraction, ensuring one-way blood flow.

questions.explanation: The mitral valve is located between the left atrium and the left ventricle. Its primary function is to prevent the backflow of blood from the left ventricle to the left atrium. When the left ventricle contracts, the mitral valve closes, ensuring that blood is directed into the aorta and not back into the left atrium. This mechanism is crucial for efficient circulation. The other options describe incorrect pathways or roles for the mitral valve, as it does not operate between the right atrium and right ventricle, nor does it involve the right atrium to left atrium flow.

questions.text: About 55% of the volume of human blood is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: About 55% of the volume of human blood is plasma. Plasma is the liquid component of blood that makes up the majority of its volume. It contains water, electrolytes, hormones, waste products, and various proteins.
Blood cells, including red blood cells (erythrocytes), white blood cells (leukocytes), and platelets, make up the remaining portion of blood volume. These cells are suspended in the plasma.
While blood proteins are a component of plasma, they do not constitute the largest proportion of blood volume.
The correct answer is A) Plasma. Blood cells (B) constitute a smaller proportion of blood volume compared to plasma.

questions.explanation: About 55% of the volume of human blood is plasma. Plasma is the liquid component of blood that makes up the majority of its volume. It contains water, electrolytes, hormones, waste products, and various proteins.

questions.text: The nature of valves in the heart is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The valves in the heart are membranous, composed of sheets of tough, flexible connective tissue called leaflets. These flaps are crucial for ensuring unidirectional blood flow through the heart.
The heart valves themselves are not muscular; they are supported by muscles such as the papillary muscles, which help in the operation of the valves, but the valve material is not muscle tissue.
Heart valves are not composed of ligamentous tissue. While ligaments may support their positioning, the valves themselves are made of membranous tissue, not ligaments.
This option is incorrect because the valves are specifically membranous, not a combination of membranous, muscular, and ligamentous.

questions.explanation: The nature of valves in the heart is membranous. They are composed of sheets of tough membrane like connective tissue (leaflets) that act as flaps. The heart has four valves:Mitral valve (bicuspid valve) - Located between the left atrium and left ventricle. It has two leaflets.Tricuspid valve - Located between the right atrium and right ventricle. It has three leaflets.Pulmonary valve - Located between the right ventricle and the pulmonary artery. It has three leaflets.Aortic valve - Located between the left ventricle and the aorta. It has three leaflets.

questions.text: The pathway adopted by water in plants through adjacent cell walls is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct. The apoplast pathway is the route taken by water in plants through the non-living spaces of adjacent cell walls. In the apoplast pathway, water moves freely through the cell walls without crossing cell membranes. It is an important pathway for the movement of water and minerals within plants.

The symplast pathway involves the movement of water and solutes through the living parts of cells, such as the cytoplasm and plasmodesmata (channels connecting adjacent cells). While the symplast pathway is crucial for the overall movement of substances within plants, it is not specifically related to water movement through adjacent cell walls.
The vacuolar pathway is not a commonly recognized term in plant physiology. The vacuole is a storage organelle within plant cells, and its role is different from that of the apoplast pathway.

The tonoplast is the membrane surrounding the vacuole and is involved in regulating the movement of substances in and out of the vacuole. However, it does not specifically refer to the pathway of water through adjacent cell walls.

questions.explanation: The apoplast pathway is the route taken by water in plants through the non-living spaces of adjacent cell walls. In the apoplast pathway, water moves freely through the cell walls without crossing cell membranes. It is an important pathway for the movement of water and minerals within plants.

questions.text: First artery that arises from the base of aorta:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pulmonary artery arises from the right ventricle, not the aorta. It carries deoxygenated blood to the lungs, making it unique among arteries.
The coronary arteries are the first branches from the base of the aorta. They supply oxygen-rich blood to the heart muscle, essential for its function.
The hepatic artery originates from the celiac trunk, which is a branch of the abdominal aorta, not directly from the base of the aorta.
The iliac arteries arise from the bifurcation of the abdominal aorta, not from the base of the aorta.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: Coronary artery. The coronary arteries are the first vessels to branch off from the base of the aorta, providing the heart muscle with its own supply of oxygen-rich blood. This is crucial for the heart's continuous function. The pulmonary artery, while significant in carrying deoxygenated blood to the lungs, arises from the right ventricle rather than the aorta. The hepatic artery originates from the celiac trunk, and the iliac arteries are formed from the division of the abdominal aorta, making them incorrect answers in this context.

questions.text: At start of ventricular contraction:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Bi and tricuspid valves are opened
options.explanation: No, the bicuspid and tricuspid valves are not opened at the start of ventricular contraction. The bicuspid and tricuspid valves are located between the atria and ventricles of the heart. They open during atrial systole, which is the contraction of the atria, to allow blood to flow from the atria to the ventricles. At the start of ventricular contraction, the ventricles are still relaxed, and the pressure in the ventricles is lower than the pressure in the atria. This keeps the bicuspid and tricuspid valves closed, preventing blood from flowing back into the atria.

The semilunar valves are closed at the start of ventricular contraction. This is because the pressure in the ventricles is still lower than the pressure in the arteries. The semilunar valves only open when the pressure in the ventricles is higher than the pressure in the arteries. The semilunar valves are located between the ventricles and the arteries. They are responsible for preventing blood from flowing back into the ventricles from the arteries.

options.text: Bi and tricuspid valves are opened and semilunar valves are closed
options.explanation: No, at the start of ventricular contraction, the bi and tricuspid valves are closed and the semilunar valves are open. The bi and tricuspid valves are located between the atria and ventricles. They allow blood to flow from the atria to the ventricles during diastole. At the start of ventricular contraction, the pressure in the ventricles increases, which closes the bi and tricuspid valves. This prevents blood from flowing back into the atria. The semilunar valves are located between the ventricles and the arteries. They allow blood to flow from the ventricles to the arteries during systole. At the start of ventricular contraction, the pressure in the ventricles is still lower than the pressure in the arteries. This keeps the semilunar valves closed. As the ventricles contract and the pressure inside them increases, the semilunar valves open and allow blood to flow out of the heart. The closing of the bi and tricuspid valves and the opening of the semilunar valves create the first heart sound. This sound is heard as a "lub-dub" sound. The "lub" sound is caused by the closing of the bi and tricuspid valves, and the "dub" sound is caused by the opening of the semilunar valves.

options.text: Bi and tricuspid valves are closed and semilunar valves are opened
options.explanation: At the start of ventricular contraction, the biventricular heart valves (mitral and tricuspid valves) are closed and the semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonic valves) are opened. This allows the ventricles to contract and pump blood out of the heart. The mitral valve connects the left atrium to the left ventricle, and the tricuspid valve connects the right atrium to the right ventricle. When these valves are closed, they prevent blood from flowing back into the atria during ventricular contraction. The aortic valve connects the left ventricle to the aorta, and the pulmonic valve connects the right ventricle to the pulmonary artery. When these valves are opened, they allow blood to flow out of the ventricles into the aorta and pulmonary artery. The opening and closing of the heart valves is controlled by the electrical activity of the heart.

questions.explanation: At the start of ventricular contraction, the biventricular heart valves (mitral and tricuspid valves) are closed and the semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonic valves) are opened. This allows the ventricles to contract and pump blood out of the heart. The mitral valve connects the left atrium to the left ventricle, and the tricuspid valve connects the right atrium to the right ventricle. When these valves are closed, they prevent blood from flowing back into the atria during ventricular contraction. The aortic valve connects the left ventricle to the aorta, and the pulmonic valve connects the right ventricle to the pulmonary artery. When these valves are opened, they allow blood to flow out of the ventricles into the aorta and pulmonary artery. The opening and closing of the heart valves is controlled by the electrical activity of the heart.

questions.text: Which valve allows blood to pass from heart to pulmonary artery?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Yes, the right semilunar valve allows blood to pass from the right ventricle of the heart to the pulmonary artery. The right semilunar valve is located at the base of the pulmonary artery, where it meets the right ventricle. It has three cusps, which are crescent-shaped flaps of tissue that open and close to allow blood to flow in one direction. When the right ventricle contracts, the pressure in the ventricle rises and forces the cusps of the right semilunar valve open. This allows blood to flow from the right ventricle to the pulmonary artery. When the right ventricle relaxes, the pressure in the ventricle drops and the cusps of the right semilunar valve close. This prevents blood from flowing back from the pulmonary artery into the right ventricle.

No, the left AV valve does not allow blood to pass from the heart to the pulmonary artery. The left AV valve, also known as the mitral valve, is located between the left atrium and the left ventricle of the heart. It opens during atrial systole, which is the contraction of the left atrium, to allow blood to flow from the left atrium to the left ventricle.

No, the left semilunar valve does not allow blood to pass from the heart to the pulmonary artery. The left semilunar valve is located between the left ventricle and the pulmonary artery. It opens during ventricular systole, which is the contraction of the left ventricle, to allow oxygen-poor blood to flow from the left ventricle to the pulmonary artery. The pulmonary artery then carries this blood to the lungs, where it will be oxygenated. The left semilunar valve closes at the end of ventricular systole, when the pressure in the left ventricle falls below the pressure in the pulmonary artery. This prevents blood from flowing back into the left ventricle.

No, the right AV valve (tricuspid valve) does not allow blood to pass from the heart to the pulmonary artery. The right AV valve allows blood to pass from the right atrium to the right ventricle. The pulmonary valve is located between the right ventricle and the pulmonary artery. It opens during ventricular systole, which is the contraction of the ventricles, to allow blood to flow from the right ventricle to the pulmonary artery. The right AV valve is made up of three leaflets, which are called the anterior leaflet, the septal leaflet, and the posterior leaflet. The leaflets are attached to the papillary muscles by chordae tendineae. The papillary muscles are attached to the walls of the right ventricle.

questions.explanation: The right semilunar valve allows blood to pass from the right ventricle of the heart to the pulmonary artery. The right semilunar valve is located at the base of the pulmonary artery, where it meets the right ventricle. It has three cusps, which are crescent-shaped flaps of tissue that open and close to allow blood to flow in one direction. When the right ventricle contracts, the pressure in the ventricle rises and forces the cusps of the right semilunar valve open. This allows blood to flow from the right ventricle to the pulmonary artery. When the right ventricle relaxes, the pressure in the ventricle drops and the cusps of the right semilunar valve close. This prevents blood from flowing back from the pulmonary artery into the right ventricle.

questions.text: Myocardium of heart shows characters:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, the myocardium of the heart does not show characteristics of smooth muscle. Smooth muscle is a type of muscle tissue that is found in the walls of hollow organs, such as the stomach, intestines, and bladder. Smooth muscle is involuntary, meaning that it is not under conscious control.

No, the myocardium of the heart does not show the characteristics of smooth and voluntary muscles.Smooth muscle is a type of muscle that is found in the walls of hollow organs, such as the intestines and blood vessels. It is not under voluntary control, meaning that we cannot consciously control its contractions. Smooth muscle is responsible for the involuntary movements of these organs, such as peristalsis in the intestines and blood flow through the blood vessels.Voluntary muscle is a type of muscle that is found in the limbs and torso. It is under voluntary control, meaning that we can consciously control its contractions. Voluntary muscle is responsible for the voluntary movements of the body, such as walking, running, and talking.

Yes, the myocardium of the heart is both striated and involuntary.Striated means that the muscle fibers have a striped appearance when viewed under a microscope. This is due to the arrangement of the thick and thin filaments that make up the contractile apparatus of the muscle. Involuntary means that the muscle is not under conscious control. The contraction of the myocardium is controlled by the autonomic nervous system. The myocardium is the muscle tissue that makes up the walls of the heart. It is responsible for pumping blood throughout the body. The myocardium is striated and involuntary, which allows it to contract and relax rhythmically and automatically to pump blood.

No, the myocardium of the heart is striated but not voluntary. Striated means that the muscle cells have a striped appearance, which is due to the arrangement of the myofibrils within the cells. Myofibrils are bundles of protein filaments that are responsible for muscle contraction. Voluntary means that the muscle can be controlled by the conscious mind. The myocardium of the heart is involuntary, which means that it cannot be controlled by the conscious mind.

questions.explanation: The myocardium of the heart is both striated and involuntary.Striated means that the muscle fibers have a striped appearance when viewed under a microscope. This is due to the arrangement of the thick and thin filaments that make up the contractile apparatus of the muscle. Involuntary means that the muscle is not under conscious control. The contraction of the myocardium is controlled by the autonomic nervous system. The myocardium is the muscle tissue that makes up the walls of the heart. questions.text: In humans, only systemic arch is present.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In humans, the systemic arch refers to the main artery that carries oxygenated blood from the heart to the rest of the body. This is the aortic arch.In humans, only the left aortic arch persists and develops into the systemic arch.
It refers to above or toward the head, and doesn’t identify any specific arch.
In some animals (e.g. birds), the right arch becomes the main systemic arch — but not in humans.
The inferior vena cava is a vein (not an artery), and it's part of venous circulation, not systemic arterial circulation.
